docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This C++ library provides simple cryptographic API with focus on RSA and AES. Built on OpenSSL, but isn't a direct wrapper to make the usage as simple as possible.

This library is MIT licensed, so you can use it however you want in your projects!

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• C++ Standard : C++11 or later
  • Dependencies : OpenSSL3.0+

docs.title: Example usage
docs.text:
Example encryption: 

#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Data buffer setup
  std::string my_message = "Hewwo I am secret ^.^";
  std::vector<unsigned char> payload(my_message.size());
  std::copy(my_message.data(),my_message.data()+my_message.size(),payload.data());

  // Keypair with key size of 2048 gets generated and our buffer gets encrypted
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key_factory;
  key_factory.generate_keypair(2048);
  std::vector<unsigned char> res = tancrypt::RSA::encrypt(key_factory,payload);

  // Check results compared original, hex x  encrypted
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << my_message << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(res) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Algorithm support

The supported OpenSSL algorithms are limited to those mapped in enum class hashAlg. This prevents errors from requesting unsupported or non-existent algorithms.

This component provides the hashing functionality required for RSA::sign and RSA::verify, but it can also be used as a standalone hasher. Supported algorithms are directly provided by OpenSSL.

Extends the namespace pkicxx without a sub-namespace.

docs.title: enum class tancrypt::hashAlg : int
docs.text: This enum class is used as hashAlg type, it allows better type safety and decreases amount of errors caused by typos

docs.title: Available algorithms
docs.text:
• hashAlg::MDC2
  • hashAlg::MD4
  • hashAlg::MD5
  • hashAlg::SHA1
  • hashAlg::RSA_SHA1
  • hashAlg::SHA224
  • hashAlg::SHA256
  • hashAlg::SHA384
  • hashAlg::SHA512
  • hashAlg::SHA512_224
  • hashAlg::SHA512_256
  • hashAlg::SM3
  • hashAlg::BLAKE2B512
  • hashAlg::BLAKE2S256

docs.title: tancrypt::hash
docs.text: Provides hashing functionality required for sign and verify functions, but can be also used independently.

docs.title: tancrypt::hash(std::vector<unsigned char>& data,hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • std::vector<unsigned char>& data - Data buffer to hash
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Algorithm to use for the digest.
  • Returns:
    • std::vector<unsigned char> data - Hashed data buffer

docs.title: _hashTypeMap
docs.text: Internal function that returns internal std::map, which maps enums to the OpenSSL algorithm names

docs.title: static const std::map<int,const char>& _hashTypeMap()
docs.text:
• Parameters:
  • Returns:
    • static const std::map<int,const char*>& - Map of enum : int values to char* names of the algorithms

docs.title: Helper functions
docs.text: Helpful utility functions, good for debugging and other processes. These functions live in namespace tancrypt.

docs.title: tancrypt::hexStr
docs.text: This function provides an easy way to convert data into string interpretation of hex values. Useful when you want to print out or compare buffers with non-string data.

docs.title: tancrypt::hexStr(const std::vector<unsigned char> &data)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • const std::vector<unsigned char> &data - The input data buffer.
  • Returns:
    • std::string - A formatted hex string (16 blocks per row).

docs.title: PKIC
docs.text:
Note

Please note that newly created instance of RSA::pkic is blank and needs to be initialized properly before it can be used.

PKIC serves as a container, which stores and manages the RSA keys. The PKIC by itself does not provide any cryptographic operations, it only provides the keys. class pkic extends the namespace RSA and is automatically included in the RSA header.

If you wish to merely generate keypairs or load keypairs and manage them, you can include tancrypt-pkic.hpp as a standalone header.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Sub-namespace tancrypt::RSA provides basic cryptographic functions and pulls in tancrypt::RSA::pkic as required dependency.

All provided functions are provided as static stateless functions, this simplifies their usage.

docs.title: RSA::encrypt(pkic& key,std::vector<unsigned char>& payload)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic key - Key container (priv/pubkey must be loaded)
    • std::vector<unsigned char>& payload - Data buffer to encrypt
  • Returns:
    • std::vector<unsigned char> - Encrypted data buffer

docs.title: Encrypt example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Data buffer setup
  std::string my_message = "Hewwo I am secret ^.^";
  std::vector<unsigned char> payload(my_message.size());
  std::copy(my_message.data(),my_message.data()+my_message.size(),payload.data());

  // Keypair with key size of 2048 gets generated and our buffer gets encrypted
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key_store;
  key_store.generate_keypair(2048);
  std::vector<unsigned char> res = tancrypt::RSA::pki::encrypt(key_store,payload);

  // Check results compared original, hex x  encrypted
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << my_message << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(res) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::decrypt(pkic& key,std::vector<unsigned char>& payload)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic& key - Key container (Must contain private key)
    • std::vector<unsigned char>& payload - Encrypted data buffer
  • Returns:
    • std::vector<unsigned char> - Decrypted data buffer

docs.title: Decrypt example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key;
  key.generate_keypair(2048);

  // Preparing encrypted data 
  std::string my_message = "Hewwo I am secret ^.^";
  std::vector<unsigned char> payload(my_message.size());
  std::copy(my_message.data(),my_message.data()+my_message.size(),payload.data()); 
  std::vector<unsigned char> res = tancrypt::RSA::pki::encrypt(key,payload);
  std::cout << "Original:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << my_message << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Hex:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(payload) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Encrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(res) << std::endl;

  // Decrypting the data again
  std::vector<unsigned char> res_decrypted = tancrypt::pki::decrypt(key,res);

  // Debug print to check that the data indeed match
  std::cout << "Res decrypted hex:"<< std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(res_decrypted) << std::endl;
  std::cout << "Res decrypted:" << std::endl;
  std::cout << res_decrypted.data() << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::sign(pkic& key,std::vector<unsigned char> &buffer, hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic key - key container (must contain private key)
    • std::vector<unsigned char> &buffer - The data to be signed
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Hashing algorithm to use for the digest
  • Returns:
    • std::vector<unsigned char> - Data signature

docs.title: Signature example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  tancrypt::RSA::pkic key_store;
  key_store.generate_keypair(2048);

  std::string message = "I confirm this transaction.";
  std::vector<unsigned char> data(message.begin(), message.end());

  // Signing the data using SHA256
  std::vector<unsigned char> signature = tancrypt::RSA::pki::sign(key_store, data, tancrypt::hashAlg::SHA256);

  std::cout << "Signature (Hex):" << std::endl;
  std::cout << tancrypt::hexStr(signature) << std::endl;

  return 0;
}

docs.title: RSA::verify
docs.text:
Note

Algorithm choice must match the algorithm which was used to sign the data, otherwise the operation will fail.

docs.title: RSA::verify(pkic& key, std::vector<unsigned char>&sig, std::vector<unsigned char> &buffer, hashAlg alg)
docs.text:
• Parameters:
    • tancrypt::RSA::pkic& key - key container (must contain public key)
    • std::vector<unsigned char> &sig - Singature data buffer
    • std::vector<unsigned char> &buffer - The data to verify
    • tancrypt::hashAlg alg - Hashing algorithm to use for the digest
  • Returns:
    • bool - Signature matches data (returns true if valid)

docs.title: Verify signature example
docs.text:
#include "tancrypt.hpp"

int main()
{
  // Assuming 'key_store' contains public key, 'signature' and 'data' contain valid data
  bool is_valid = tancrypt::RSA::pki::verify(key_store, signature, data, tancrypt::hashAlg::SHA256);

  if (is_valid) {
      std::cout << "Signature is authentic!" << std::endl;
  } else {
      std::cout << "Signature verification failed." << std::endl;
  }

  return 0;
}

docs.title: Packages
docs.text: The lib can be also acquired from following repos.

docs.title: Arch linux
docs.text: AUR: yay -S pkicxx

docs.title: Adding library as dependency
docs.text:
You can simply add it as your dependency into lib/. 

cd lib/
git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt
Now either add it as subdirectory into your cmake or build manually using: 
cd lib/
git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt && cd pkicxx/build
cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. && cmake --build .

docs.title: Installing as system lib
docs.text:
You can install the library to your system and keep it as shared lib. To install use following commands:

git clone https://github.com/Sora-3e8/tancrypt && cd pkicxx/build
cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release .. && cmake --build .
sudo cmake --install .
Now you can link against it as if it was normal system library. The library should be findable by cmake.
